name: Tichborne
coordsSource: web-verified:getthedata-SO24-0ND-Tichborne(51.068986,-1.186771);historic-england-Church-of-St-Andrew(51.0689,-1.1897 ~150m);cross-check listing centroid 51.07975,-1.16158 ~1.7km NE polluted; stored 51.0637,-1.153 ~2.4km SE rejected; 2026-06-04
overview: Tichborne is a small, historic village and civil parish about 4 miles east of Winchester and 2 miles south-west of Alresford in Hampshire, set across the water meadows of the infant River Itchen, with a population of around 167. It is famous for two things: the Tichborne Dole, one of England's oldest charitable customs (dating from around 1150, when Lady Mabella Tichborne endowed an annual gift of flour), and the Victorian 'Tichborne Claimant' impostor case. The Saxon-chancelled church of St Andrew is rare in containing a Roman Catholic chapel within a pre-Reformation Anglican parish church.
character: A tiny, picturesque scattering of thatched houses in rolling farmland, steeped in centuries of tradition around the long-established Tichborne family.
amenities: The Tichborne Arms (thatched country pub, site licensed since 1487)
St Andrew's Church (Saxon chancel; shared Anglican/RC chapel)
Tichborne Dole charitable festival (Lady Day, 25 March)
Alresford Golf Course (founded 1890) covering the north-east of the parish
Surrounding Itchen water meadows and footpaths

prices.summary: A tiny, sought-after village; period and thatched cottages and farmhouses rarely come to market, supporting high values when they do. See Rightmove for current sold-price detail.

thingsToDo: St Andrew's Church with its Tichborne family monuments
Walks across the Itchen water meadows
placesToEat: Pubs and cafes in nearby Alresford and Cheriton
pros: A genuinely historic village with a famous living tradition
Beautiful Itchen-valley setting close to Winchester
cons: Very small with minimal everyday amenities (no shop or school)
Few homes ever come to market
whoItSuits: Buyers wanting a tiny, tradition-rich Itchen-valley village with a great pub, close to Winchester and Alresford.
